Choose from: an ecosystem simulation (predators, prey, and plants on a grid), a traffic intersection simulation (cars, traffic lights, pedestrians), or a bank simulation (customers, tellers, queues). The simulation should model entities as classes, run for a configurable number of time steps, and produce statistics about system behavior over time. This project bridges CS101 OOP fundamentals with the deeper OOP patterns you will study in CS102.
objectives: Design a class hierarchy that accurately models a real-world system with multiple interacting entities
Apply encapsulation to protect entity state and enforce valid transitions
Use composition to build complex objects from simpler ones
Implement a simulation loop that advances time and updates all entities
Collect and report statistics about system behavior over time
deliverables: Python source files organized by class (one file per major class or logical group)
Main simulation runner with configurable parameters (time steps, entity counts, initial conditions)
Output showing simulation progress and final statistics (population over time, throughput, wait times, etc.)
Class diagram (hand-drawn or digital) showing all classes and their relationships
Design document (1-2 pages) explaining modeling decisions and simplifications

criteria: At least 3 distinct classes with meaningful attributes and methods
Classes use encapsulation — state changes go through methods, not direct attribute modification
Composition is used where appropriate (e.g., an Ecosystem contains a list of Animal objects)
Simulation runs correctly for configurable time steps and produces consistent results
Classes have clear responsibilities and do not mix concerns
__init__, __str__, and other special methods are implemented where appropriate
Methods are well-named and have docstrings
Code follows consistent style and naming conventions throughout
Simulation parameters are configurable without code changes
Statistics are collected efficiently during the simulation, not computed after the fact
Output is formatted clearly with aligned columns and appropriate precision
The simulation handles edge cases (all entities die, queue empties, etc.) gracefully
Class diagram accurately reflects the implementation
Design document explains what real-world aspects are modeled and what is simplified
README explains how to run the simulation and what the output means
Code comments explain non-obvious design decisions
Well-designed class hierarchy demonstrating OOP fundamentals
Working simulation that models entity interactions over time
Clear statistics output showing system behavior trends
Complete documentation including class diagram and design rationale

major_assignments.title: Final Portfolio Project
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a complete Python application of your choosing that integrates concepts from the entire course. The project must demonstrate proficiency in variables and data types, control flow, functions, data structures (lists, dicts, sets), file I/O, error handling, modules, OOP, and at least basic algorithmic thinking. Students propose their project idea in Week 13 for instructor approval. The final deliverable is a polished, documented application with a presentation component. Example projects: a personal finance tracker, a quiz/flashcard application, a recipe manager, a contact book with search, or a simple game with persistent high scores.
objectives: Synthesize all course concepts into a cohesive, functional application
Demonstrate independent design and implementation skills on a self-directed project
Produce code of professional quality with proper organization, documentation, and error handling
Present and explain technical decisions to an audience of peers and instructor
Practice the complete software development lifecycle: design, implement, test, document, present
deliverables: Complete Python application source code organized into modules/packages
Project proposal (1 page) submitted Week 13 for approval
User guide explaining how to install dependencies, run the application, and use all features
Technical document (2-3 pages) explaining architecture, design decisions, and lessons learned
5-minute presentation with live demonstration to the class in Week 15

criteria: Application uses at least 3 data structures (lists, dicts, sets, or custom classes) appropriately
File I/O is used for data persistence — application state survives between runs
Error handling covers all user-facing operations with informative messages
OOP is used meaningfully — at least 2 classes with attributes, methods, and encapsulation
Code is organized into multiple modules with clear responsibilities
Functions and methods are small, well-named, and documented with docstrings
Consistent coding style throughout the entire project
No dead code, no commented-out code, no leftover debug prints
Application handles edge cases and unexpected input without crashing
Algorithms are appropriate for the data sizes involved
The application is responsive — no unnecessary delays or inefficient operations
Resource management (files, connections) uses context managers properly
User guide enables a peer to install and use the application without additional help
Technical document demonstrates understanding of design trade-offs and decisions
Presentation clearly communicates the project's purpose, design, and demonstration
Code documentation (docstrings, comments) is comprehensive and accurate
Complete, polished application integrating all major course concepts
Professional code quality with modular organization and comprehensive documentation
Robust error handling and graceful edge case management throughout
Clear, confident presentation with live demonstration of all major features

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes throughout the semester testing understanding of programming concepts, syntax, and problem-solving ability

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on programming exercises reinforcing each week's lecture topics with practical coding tasks

major_assignments.description: Four major projects of increasing complexity integrating multiple programming concepts

midterm_examination.description: Written and coding exam in Week 8 covering variables, control flow, functions, strings, and lists

final_project.description: Comprehensive final portfolio project demonstrating mastery of all course concepts with presentation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged, but all code must be written independently. Sharing code or copying from any source without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted code must be your own work. Use of AI code generators must be disclosed. Plagiarism or unauthorized collaboration will result in a zero on the assignment and possible course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with Python 3.12+ and VS Code installed. Lab computers are available but personal machines are strongly recommended for practice outside class.
